title: Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases
subtitle: The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.
caption: Across the globe on February 22, 2026, the biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases displays a fragmented distribution of rising and falling infection rates, with the majority of nations showing no available data. The map utilizes a diverging color scale ranging from dark blue, representing a 100% decrease in cases, to dark red, representing a 100% increase, with a neutral center at 0% change. The most significant declines in case numbers appear in the Americas and parts of Europe, where Peru and Guatemala are shaded in the darkest blue band, indicating a drop of 75% to 100% compared to the previous two weeks. Argentina and Mexico also show substantial decreases, falling within the -50% to -75% range. In Europe, a similar downward trend is visible in Greece, Italy, and the United Kingdom, all situated in the -25% to -50% band. Conversely, positive growth in confirmed cases is concentrated in specific regions of South America and Northern Europe. Brazil and Guyana are shaded in light orange, placing them in the 0% to 25% increase category, while Sweden and Estonia show slightly higher growth rates in the 25% to 50% band. The vast majority of the world's landmass, including the United States, Canada, Russia, China, Australia, and the entirety of Africa, is marked with diagonal hatching to signify that no data is reported for these territories on this date. The visual pattern highlights a distinct geographic split where South American and European nations dominate the available data points, showing either sharp contractions or moderate expansions in case counts, while large continental blocks remain unmeasured.
